---
title: "Highly Interpretable Prediction Models for SNP Data"
author: "Prof. Dr. Robin Nunkesser"
format: revealjs
---

# Highly Interpretable Prediction Models for SNP Data

# Introduction

## Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms (SNPs)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Common variation at a single position in a DNA sequence among individuals
- Two possible alleles resulting in three possible genotypes
    + homozygous A
    + homozygous B 
    + heterozygous AB

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

![](../../images/talks/black/Dna-SNP.png)
<span style='font-size: xx-small;'>By SNP model by David Eccles (gringer), <a href="https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0" title="Creative Commons Attribution 4.0">CC BY 4.0</a>, <a href="https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=2355125">Link</a></span>

:::
::::

---


## Interpretability 

> An interpretable model is [...] following a domain-specific set of constraints that make reasoning processes understandable. [...] 
> Logical models [...] are among the most popular algorithms [...], since their statements provide human-understandable reasons for each prediction.

<span style='font-size: xx-small;'>Rudin et al. (2021) Interpretable Machine Learning</span>

## Domain-specific constraints

> It is well-known that in the analysis of genetic features such as SNPs, interactions, e.g., gene-gene interactions [...] and gene-environment interactions [...], play a crucial role.

<span style='font-size: xx-small;'>Lau et al. (2024) logicDT: a procedure for identifying response‑associated interactions between binary predictors</span>

## Case Control Studies on Gene-Gene Interactions 

$SNP_1$  | $SNP_2$  | $\ldots$ | $SNP_P$  | Case
-------- | -------- | -------- | -------- | --
0        | 2        | $\ldots$ | 1        | 0
1        | 2        | $\ldots$ | 0        | 0
$\ldots$ | $\ldots$ | $\ldots$ | $\ldots$ | $\ldots$
2        | 0        | $\ldots$ | 0        | 1

# State of the Art

## Microsoft's AutoML

- AveragedPerceptron
- SdcaLogisticRegression
- SdcaNonCalibrated
- SymbolicSgdLogisticRegression
- LbfgsLogisticRegression
- LightGbm
- FastTree
- FastForest
- Gam
- FieldAwareFactorizationMachine
- Prior
- LinearSvm

## logicDT

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Decision tree based method 
- High interpretability
- High prediction performance
- Necessity of hyperparameter optimization

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

![](../../images/talks/black/logicDTscrime_7.svg)

:::
::::

# Further Established Learning Methods

## logicFS

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

- Reports important interactions
- High interpretability
- Interactions are considered seperately

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

![](../../images/talks/black/logicFSscrime.svg)

:::
::::


## GPAS / logicGP

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

- GPAS is a special case of the unreleased logicGP
- Models based on Disjunctive Normal Form of logical expressions
- High interpretability
- No automatic model selection

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

![](../../images/talks/black/interactions_pruned.svg)

:::
::::

# Automatic Model Selection

## Algorithm for GPAS

![](../../images/talks/black/modelselection.svg)

## Consolidation

- Discard all models that do only appear in one run on the folds
- Discard all models where a model of the same size with a better average accuracy on the validation data exists

## Selection

- Discard models that do not at least offer $1\%$ more accuracy than all smaller models
- Choose the model with the greatest accuracy

# Experiments

## Simulated Data

- Standard simulation of ```R``` package ```scrime```
- 100 runs on training and test data with 1.000 observations each

![](../../images/talks/black/scrime.svg)

## Results

![](../../images/talks/black/gpasacc.svg)

# logicGP

## Introduction

- logicGP is a generalization of GPAS
- Multiclass classification is possible
- Offers better predictions for interactions with less SNPs
- Future versions will enable the analysis of gene-environment interactions

## Example Model

$w_{Control}$ | $w_{Case}$    | Condition
------------- | ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------
1.38          | 0.74          | $SNP_6 \in \{1\}$
0.35          | 1.78          | $(SNP_9 \in \{1\})(SNP_3 \in \{1\})(SNP_10 \in \{1\})$
0.74          | 1.35          | $SNP_7 \in \{1\}$
0.62          | 0.38          | All above false

## First Results

![](../../images/talks/black/gpasaccgrouped.svg)




# Thank you for your attention!
